utterances: (Soundbite of music)
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.

We're talking today about tracking hurricanes and the difficulties of doing so, especially when the budget of the agencies that's tasked with that--to perform that deed has held steady for many years.
Our guest is Debbie Cenziper, a reporter for The Miami Herald.
That newspaper's conducted an eight-month investigation into every hurricane that's struck land since Hurricane Andrew 13 years ago.
And, Debbie, talk to us a little bit about this funding problem.
How much money does the government spend on the National Hurricane Center?

You know, I didn't really get into the Hurricane Center's operating budget, but I do know that the number of forecasters at the Hurricane Center has not grown in years even though, you know, we are dealing with a busier, you know, more active hurricane season.
What I dealt with was more the equipment, and that's not maintained by the Hurricane Center.
It's maintained by the National Weather Service and NOAA, and so we've kind of looked at trends with the equipment and what they've put money into and what they haven't over time.
Talk to us a little bit about how the Hurricane Center relates to those two other services you were talking about, the National Weather Service and NOAA, the National Oceanic & Atmospheric agency.
Sure.
NOAA, it goes--it's the Department of Commerce.
That's kind of the umbrella agency.
NOAA is within the Department of Commerce.
The Weather Service reports to NOAA and the Hurricane Center reports to the Weather Service.
And the Weather Service is responsible for maintaining some of this equipment out in the field, if you will, these buoys, and these are often dropped buoys that, you say, malfunction a lot.

Yeah, they're not really dropped, they're moored buoys, and what happens is, first of all, it's hard--when they break or a ship--you know, they're hit by a ship or for whatever reason they malfunction, it's hard to get out there, you know.
They're in the ocean.
And so delays can take a long time.
So what we found is even during the hurricane season, buoys have been broken at crucial times and, you know, there's just not a lot of money to maintain them.
The Weather Service waits for free rides from the Coast Guard instead of maybe chartering a ship or, you know, getting out there more quickly.
So it's really just an issue of money and priorities.
One of the biggest improvements over the past few years is radar, Doppler radar.
That's much more effective than previous systems.
That's right.
Yeah.
In fact, Congress spent a lot of money in the 1990s to upgrade the nation's radars.
In fact, they spent more than a billion dollars to do this.

But what's happening at some radars--in fact, it happened twice in Miami last summer, is, you know, the radars are getting hit--they're getting struck by lightning and it's disabling them at critical times, so there is a plan to install fiber-optic wiring, cabling on the radars which would help better protect them, but it's $3 1/2 million and so far the Weather Service hasn't come up with the money.
Let's get some listeners involved in the conversation.
Again, (800) 989-8255, if you'd like to join us.
Jim is on the line, Jim from Pensacola, Florida.
Good afternoon.
I enjoy your program, Neal, very much.
Thank you.

While listening to Debbie describe some of the deficiencies within the budget, I just--you know, I'm trying to figure out, you know, to what extent that has affected, if at all, our subject to making decisions for evacuating or preparing for, you know, hurricanes.

It seems to me as being a Floridian--native Floridian that we have ample notice, ample warning, and a tremendous, you know, system in terms of advancing information regarding these hurricanes, and to suggest that our response to that could be better with more money, I just don't see it.
Debbie, do you...
Oh, yeah.
...have examples of how it might be better with more money?
What's the problem, I think Jim is asking.
Sure.
I mean it's happened, you know, time and time again in recent years and, in fact, dating back to Andrew in '92 where lapses in basic equipment potentially affected forecasts.
Like I said, during Charley last year, they anticipated a Category 2 hurricane at--you know, it turned into a Category 4, caught a lot of people by surprise.
It shifted east at the last minute, again catching people by surprise, and there was, you know, a whole bunch of equipment out at that moment.
So nobody is suggesting that the Hurricane Center is not doing a good job.
They are, you know, among some of the best meteorologists in the country.
The question is, could they be doing it better if they had what they needed, and forecasters all over at the Weather Service offices all over the East Coast talk about these problems.
Let's bring another voice in on this question.
Joining us now is Mike Black.
He's a research meteorologist at the National Oceanic & Atmospheric Administration, and he also joins us from the studios at The Miami Herald.
And, Mike, good to have you on the program.
Well, I share his sentiments.
I think overall the forecasts are superb.
The Hurricane Center does an excellent job.
But as Debbie points out, could they do better, and I think the question to that is absolutely.
And I think the Hurricane Center would be the first to admit that, and they're always striving to do better.
And it's not only the equipment.

There's some question on any individual case of what equipment, how much it would affect it, and you basically don't know because it wasn't available so you can't go back and see what difference a forecast would make.
But there's other areas, and particularly research that I'm involved in, that can help the forecasts.
Could these sudden shifts, these turns to the east like Charley did or to the south like Katrina did in south Florida, could they be better anticipated?
Personally I believe they can.
It's gonna take more research.
It's gonna take the next generations of models that NOAA is working on, but frankly, does not have the resources to accelerate that program, and so the outcome of that is in doubt right now.
So I think we could do better, and certainly that should be the goal of NOAA to get the best possible forecasts out there for the American public.
So these research projects on hurricanes, they are being starved of funding, you're saying?
Yes, certainly some of them.
I work for the Hurricane Research Division here on Virginia Key in Miami.

We're part of another laboratory, the Atlantic Oceanographic and Meteorological Laboratory at NOAA, and we were designated by Congress back in the 1950s as a national laboratory to specifically study hurricanes and to improve the forecasts, and over the last two decades or so our budget--amount of dollars we get every year, a steady income, has remained essentially flat, and so inflation and rising costs have taken their toll, and what the net result is that our staff has dwindled and we're no longer able to really fully do our mission anymore, which is to advance the science of hurricane forecasting, help out the Hurricane Center in any way we possibly can.
And it has been problems.
I think we can do a lot better than that.
We are keying in on three areas of research that's been mentioned here already.
One is intensity.
We have very little skill in predicting how strong storms are gonna be when they make landfall.

There have been great strides made in track forecasting, and you've seen that this year with the excellent forecasts that were made in--for Katrina in particular, for the Louisiana-Mississippi landfall.
And then the last is rainfall, and that you mentioned at the beginning of the show, talking about Hurricane Stan and the devastating effects in Central America.
And that we've barely scratched the surface, as my boss, Frank Marks, the director of the Hurricane Research Division, has said.
There's a lot more that we can do, but we simply don't have the funding, the staff available to analyze the data, to look into the details and provide the science that's needed.
Jim, does that answer your question?
I think Jim's left us.
Anyway, I thank him for his call.
By the way, we should mention that we had invited representatives from NOAA and the National Weather Service to join us today.
They declined to do so.
Let's get another caller on the line, and this is John, and John's calling from Doylestown, Pennsylvania.
Yes, hi, Neal.
Hi, Debbie.
Hi.

I was wondering if Debbie could talk about the relationship between data gathering and the computer model and the computer program, because I think a lot of people don't understand that the model and the program are both highly dependant on data gathering, and the more data points you have, the more accurate it can be.
That's right.

I'm not a meteorologist or an expert, but--I'm gonna let Mike answer that--but from what I've been told, when there are gaps in the data, when there are gaps in weather balloon data and Drop Wind Sonde datas, that that can affect the computer models.
Drop Wind Sondes?
What are those?
Drop Wind Sondes are released from hurricane hunter planes into the depths of storms and they provide all kinds of data that feed into the computer models.
And the failure rate of Drop Wind Sondes in the last few years has been about 50 percent.
They're failing...
Wow.
...at the surface of a storm.
So that's, you know, information that's denied to the computer models.
Mike Black, maybe you can give us a little bit more on the relationship between the amount of data available and the accuracy of the tracking and intensity?

Well, a computer model is only as good as the data that goes into it, and the Weather Service uses some of the most sophisticated computer models in the world, both to track the atmosphere as a whole, as well as they have a few very limited models of hurricanes.
NOAA's working very hard on the next generation hurricane model to specifically simulate the actual conditions of a hurricane.
That means the rainfall and wind patterns that we actually observe.
For that you need accurate observations.
Mike Black is a research meteorologist at the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, and he joined us from the studios at The Miami Herald.
We'll continue to take your call as we switch the focus on maybe to the politics of hurricane prediction when we come back from a short break.
We'll also be talking more about Saturday's referendum in Iraq.
We'll go to Kurdistan.
I'm Neal Conan.
It's TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
(Announcements)
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
And here are the headlines from some of the stories we're following here today at NPR News.
The United Nations has warned that measles and waterborne diseases such as cholera and diarrhea could begin to spread quickly among victims of South Asia's devastating earthquake.
Few people there have access to clean drinking water now.
But it's more than that.
You need a way to get it into the model.
You can't just simply throw a bunch of numbers into a computer model and expect the computer model to spit out the right answer.
It's a very complicated science of data assimilation to get the data correctly into the models.
You actually can make a model forecast worse by putting in additional data.
So this has to be done in a step-by-step-wise fashion and done very carefully.
We've proven we have a very good track record with the NOAA G-4 jet that NOAA purchased back in 1997.
Became operational then.

And releasing these Drop Wind Sondes in the environment of storms, the steering currents, and that data is fed into models, and at the Hurricane Research Division we have a Dr. Simm Abramson(ph) who's working with the Environmental Prediction Center in Washington, DC, on this idea of data assimilation and targeting specific regions of the atmosphere that are most sensitive to changes, and that would help better predict the course of the hurricane.
And it's made huge differences in the forecasts of the track of hurricanes, something on the order of 20 percent.
Mm-hmm.
And yet, the National Hurricane Center points out that virtually every hurricane including Charley, which we talked about earlier, did make landfall within their warning zone.
They also say that there's overlap in their system so that one malfunctioning radar or buoy here or there shouldn't make that much difference.
Yeah.
I think the question is, you know: If your warning areas are large, how do you miss?

And that's kind of what we point out in today's story, that, you know, if their forecasts were more precise, if they had more confidence in their forecast, perhaps they could narrow down the size of the warnings.
We're talking about predicting hurricanes and difficulties caused by old and malfunctioning equipment and, some say, underfunding at the National Hurricane Center in Miami.
